evidence: Affluence strongly resonates with individual-scale material abundance (10/10) and functional value (8/10). It has ethical implications (5/10) and destructive potential (5/10). Persistence over time (5/10) and predictable patterns (5/10) are key. Most other slots score 0 as they are either irrelevant or not central to the concept of wealth as defined.

Affluent strongly resonates with material possessions (ontological.material), semantic meaning (ontological.semantic), and its identification as a named concept (epistemological.identification). It catalyzes change (interconnection.catalytic), reflects core essence (resonance.essential), and ties to existential value (resonance.existential). Its destructive potential (symmetry.destructive) and generative capacity (symmetry.generative) highlight wealth's dual role. Aesthetic and ethical dimensions (value_generation) reflect how wealth intersects with beauty and morality.

Comfortable strongly resonates with material possessions (ontological.material 9), individual experience (scale.individual 8, epistemological.experience 8), and the semantic value of wealth (ontological.semantic 7). It ties to predictable outcomes (lawfulness.predictable 8) and functional utility (value_generation.functional 6). Resonance.essential (7) reflects comfort as a core aspect of wealth's value.

The word 'easy' resonates most with the functional utility of wealth (functional value), material layer (tangible possessions), and individual experience of abundance. It suggests predictable attainment (predictable) and potential excess (destructive symmetry), while superficial resonance reflects surface-level simplicity.

Catalyst (6) and resonance (7) highlight its systemic impact. Aesthetic (8) and functional (7) scores reflect its practical and symbolic roles. Destructive symmetry (5) ties to greed, while essential resonance (8) underscores its core value in wealth systems.

Hoarded wealth strongly resonates with its material nature (ontological.material), individual focus (scale.individual), and destructive excess (symmetry.destructive). It persists over time (temporal.continuation) and is perceived as tangible (epistemological.perception). Ethical implications (value.ethical) and the essential core of hoarding (resonance.essential) further highlight its resonance.

Inherited wealth strongly resonates with temporal continuation (passed through generations), scale.individual (received by a person), ontological.material (possessions), interconnection.catalytic (triggers life changes), resonance.essential (core family legacy), symmetry.cyclical (generational cycles), and value.ethical (moral implications).

Its superficial resonance (resonance.superficial) and ties to destructive excess (symmetry.destructive) reflect its dual nature as both a surface-level indulgence and a potential pathology.

Luxuriousness strongly resonates with material possessions (tangible wealth) and aesthetic value. It connects to relational dynamics (status symbols) and structural patterns (wealth distribution). It catalyzes social dynamics (interconnection.catalytic) and reflects predictable wealth patterns (lawfulness.predictable).
